data.title: Dragonfly Partner: Why are new public blockchains met with criticism as soon as they are launched?
data.description:
Author: Haseeb, Managing Partner of Dragonfly; Translator: @Jinse Finance xz

I used to warn entrepreneurs: the feedback you get after your project launches won't be hatred, but indifference. By default, nobody cares about your new public chain.

But now I have to stop saying that. Monad just launched this week, and I've never seen a newly launched blockchain suffer so much hostility. I've been investing in the crypto space for over seven years. Before 2023, almost every new public chain I saw received either enthusiastic or indifferent feedback. But now, new public chains are accompanied by a barrage of criticism from the moment they're born. Projects like Monad, Tempo, and MegaETH even faced a lot of verbal attacks before their mainnet launch—this is indeed a new phenomenon.

I've been trying to understand: why is this happening now? What market psychology does it reveal?

I've been trying to understand: why is this happening now? What market psychology does it reveal?

...

1、The Cure is Worse Than the Disease

Disclaimer: This will be the least metrics-heavy blockchain valuation article you've ever read. I'm not trying to sell you any fancy metrics or charts. Instead, I'm going to refute the prevailing sentiment in the crypto Twitter world—one I've been on the opposite side of for the past few years.

Back in 2024, I felt like I was fighting against financial nihilism. This view held that all assets were irrelevant, ultimately just memes, and everything we built was essentially worthless.

Thankfully, that atmosphere has dissipated. We broke that spell.

But the prevailing sentiment now I call financial cynicism: Okay, maybe some things do have value, maybe not all of them are memes, but they're severely overvalued, and it's only a matter of time before Wall Street discovers the truth.

Not all public blockchains are worthless, but they might only be worth 1/5 to 1/10 of their current trading price (have you ever seen those P/E ratios?), so you'd better pray Wall Street doesn't expose our bluff, or everything will be for naught. Many bullish analysts are now trying to counter this sentiment by constructing optimistic layer-one network valuation models, inflating P/E ratios, gross margins, and discounted cash flow. Late last year, Solana proudly adopted REV as the metric that would finally justify its valuation. They proudly announced: We—and only we—are no longer bluffing Wall Street! However, almost immediately after REV was adopted, it plummeted (though it's worth noting that SOL outperformed REV).

It's not that the REV metric itself is problematic. It is indeed a very clever metric. But the focus of this article is not on metric selection.

Then Hyperliquid appeared. This decentralized exchange has real revenue, a buyback mechanism, and a P/E ratio. So everyone exclaimed—see, we told you so! Finally, the first token with real profits and a reasonable P/E ratio has appeared (don't mention BNB, we won't discuss it). Hyperliquid will devour everything because it's obvious that Ethereum and Solana aren't making money, and we no longer need to pretend they have valuations.

...

Hyperliquid, Pump, and Sky, these tokens that emphasize buybacks, are all excellent. But the market has always had a channel to invest in exchanges—you can always buy Coinbase stock, BNB, or other assets. We hold HYPE tokens and acknowledge that it's an excellent product.

But that's not the reason people invest in ETH and SOL. The fact that a single-layer network doesn't offer exchange-level profit margins is not their motivation for buying—if they were looking for profit, they could have bought Coinbase stock.

If you think this article isn't a critique of blockchain financial metrics, you might guess I'm condemning the original sin of the token industry complex.

Clearly, everyone has lost money on tokens over the past year, including venture capitalists. Altcoins have performed poorly this year. Therefore, the other half of the mainstream voice on crypto Twitter is debating who to blame—who got greedy? Was it the greed of venture capitalists? Wintermute? Binance? Farmers?

Or is it the founders' greed? The answer remains the same: everyone is greedy. Venture capitalists, Wintertermute, liquidity miners, Binance, KOLs, and even yourself—no one is exempt. But that doesn't matter, because a properly functioning market never requires anyone to act against their own interests. If our assessment of the crypto industry is correct, investments can still succeed even if everyone is greedy. Trying to analyze a declining market by identifying "greedy individuals" is futile, akin to conducting a witch trial. I dare say no one will suddenly become greedy in 2025. Therefore, this is not the topic of this article. Many people expect me to write an article arguing why MON should be valued at X or MEGA at Y. But I don't want to write such an article, nor do I advise you to buy any particular asset. In fact, if you don't believe in them, you probably shouldn't invest at all. Will the new chain challenge succeed? Nobody knows. However, if there is indeed a possibility of success, the market will price it accordingly. If Ethereum is worth $300 billion or Solana is worth $80 billion, then a project with a 1%-5% probability of becoming the next Ethereum or Solana will have its price reflect that expected probability. What's puzzling is that the crypto Twitter community is outraged by this, but it's no different from the biotechnology field. A drug with less than a 10% chance of curing Alzheimer's disease, even if there's a 90% chance it won't pass Phase III clinical trials and will become worthless, will still be valued at billions of dollars by the market. This is a manifestation of mathematical logic—the market is, as it turns out, very good at calculating. Binary results are based on probability pricing, not growth rates or moral flaws. This belongs to the valuation philosophy of the "shut up and calculate" school. I think discussing this issue is pointless. "5% chance of success? Impossible, that's clearly 10%!" For any individual token, the market, not the article, is the best evaluator. Therefore, what this article truly explores is: the crypto Twitter community seems to have lost faith in the value of public blockchains. I believe this isn't because they question the ability of new public blockchains to gain market share—we just witnessed Solana rise from the ashes and dominate the market in less than two years. This, while not easy, is certainly possible. The deeper reason lies in the growing belief that even if a new public blockchain wins, there's no worthwhile prize to fight for. If ETH is just a meme and will never generate real revenue, then even if you win, you won't achieve a $300 billion valuation. This race isn't worth participating in because all valuations are bubbles that will burst before you claim your reward. Optimism about public blockchain valuations seems out of place. It's not that no one is optimistic—obviously, there are bullish voices in the market. Where there's a seller, there's a buyer. Despite the crypto Twitter influencers' enthusiasm for predicting the downfall of layer one networks, some are still confidently buying SOL at $140 and ETH at $3,000. However, a prevailing belief is that the smartest people have already stopped buying smart contract public chains. The smart people know the trick has been exposed, either now or soon. Those buying now are just suckers—ride-hailing drivers, people like Tom Lee, and KOLs who constantly talk about "trillion-dollar market capitalization." Perhaps the US Treasury. But definitely not smart money. This is pure nonsense. I don't believe it, and you shouldn't either. Therefore, I feel it's necessary to write a "Manifesto of the Smart," explaining the value of general-purpose public chains. This article is not about Monad or MegaETH; it's actually a defense of ETH and SOL. Because if you accept the value of ETH and SOL, the rest of the reasoning falls into place.

Defending the valuations of ETH and SOL is not my job as a venture capitalist, but since no one else is willing to do it, I'll take the plunge.

2, Perceiving Exponential Growth

My partner, Bo, experienced the Chinese internet boom firsthand as a venture capitalist. Although I've become numb to the notion that "the crypto industry is like the internet," his stories always remind me of the high cost of misjudging trends.

He often tells a story: In the early 21st century, when all the early e-commerce venture capitalists (at the time, it was just a small circle) gathered for coffee, they debated the size of the e-commerce market.

Will it mainly sell electronics (perhaps only geeks use computers)? Can it attract female users (perhaps they value tactile experience more)? What about fresh food (perishable goods might be unmanageable)?

For early-stage venture capitalists, the answers to these questions directly determine investment direction and valuation logic. The result, of course, proved them all wildly wrong. E-commerce would sell everything, targeting customers worldwide. But at the time, nobody truly believed it, and even those who did dared not voice it because it seemed so absurd. Only by waiting long enough would exponential growth materialize. Even among those who believed in e-commerce at the time, very few thought it would reach its later scale. And those few who remained steadfast and held on became almost all billionaires. Other venture capitalists—who, according to Bo, were among them—sold out prematurely. Today, in the crypto space, believing in exponential growth is outdated. But I believe in the exponential law of the crypto world. Because I've experienced it firsthand. When I first entered the industry, nobody used these technologies: they were small-scale, riddled with bugs, and offered a terrible user experience. The total value locked on-chain is only a few million dollars. When we invested in first-generation DeFi projects—MakerDAO, Compound, and 1inch—they were still experimental products. I used EtherDelta, which had a daily trading volume of only a few million dollars, and that was considered a huge success at the time. Today, the daily trading volume on-chain routinely exceeds tens of billions of dollars. I used to think it was absurd that Tether's issuance exceeded 1 billion and it was reported by the New York Times as a Ponzi scheme on the verge of collapse; now stablecoins have a market capitalization of over $300 billion and are regulated by the Federal Reserve. I believe in exponential growth because I have witnessed it time and time again. But you might argue: stablecoin growth may be exponential, and so may DeFi trading volume, but the value hasn't been deposited into ETH or SOL. Public chains have failed to capture this value. To that, my answer is: you still don't believe in the exponential law. Because the answer to exponential growth remains the same: it doesn't matter. The future scale will far exceed today's imagination; when the great changes arrive, economies of scale will compensate for everything. See the image below. This is Amazon's profit and loss statement from 1995 to 2019, spanning 24 years. The red line represents revenue, and the gray line represents profit. See that small upward curve at the end of the gray line? That's when Amazon truly began to turn a profit in its 22nd year. When this gray line, representing net profit, first moved above zero, Amazon had already been established for 22 years. Every year prior, columnists, critics, and short sellers claimed Amazon was a Ponzi scheme that would never be profitable. Ethereum, meanwhile, had just turned 10. Here's how Amazon's stock performed over its first decade: A decade of turmoil. During this time, Amazon was constantly surrounded by skeptics and critics: Was e-commerce just a venture-funded charity? Who cared if they dumped cheap, low-quality goods on bargain hunters? How could they possibly make money like Walmart or General Electric? If you were arguing about Amazon's P/E ratio back then, you chose the wrong paradigm—it was the linear growth paradigm. But e-commerce isn't a linear trend, so everyone arguing about P/E ratios for the past 22 years has been wildly wrong. No matter how much you bid, no matter when you buy, your bullishness is far from sufficient. Because that's the law of exponential growth. For truly exponential technology, no matter how large you think it can reach, it will always exceed your imagination. This is precisely what Silicon Valley has always understood better than Wall Street. Silicon Valley grew through exponential growth, while Wall Street is steeped in linear thinking. In the past few years, the center of gravity of the crypto world has shifted from Silicon Valley to Wall Street—you can feel the change. Admittedly, crypto growth isn't as smooth as e-commerce growth. It's more explosive, with intermittent leaps. This is because crypto involves currency, is deeply tied to macro forces, and faces a more intense regulatory tug-of-war than e-commerce. Encryption strikes at the heart of a nation's economy—currency—making it more unsettling to governments than e-commerce. But the inevitability of exponential growth remains undiminished. This is a crude argument, but if encryption truly represents exponential growth, then this crude argument is correct. Zooming out, financial assets crave freedom. They crave openness. They crave interconnectivity. Encryption transforms financial assets into file formats, making sending dollars or stocks as simple as sending a PDF. It allows everything to communicate with each other, creating a 24/7, global, interconnected open ecosystem. Openness will ultimately prevail. This is a core principle I learned from the internet age. The vested interests will resist, governments will bluff, but ultimately they will succumb to the ubiquity, creativity, and extreme efficiency this technology brings. Just as the internet reshaped all industries, blockchain will similarly devour the entire financial and monetary system. Yes—given enough time—it will devour everything. There's an old saying: people always overestimate what will change in two years and underestimate what will change in ten. If you believe in exponential growth, if you broaden your perspective sufficiently, everything will still seem cheap. Every day, holders outlast sellers and detractors—this should keep you humble. Big capital has a much longer time horizon than the swing traders on crypto Twitter might convince you of. History has taught big capital not to fight against major technological changes. Remember that grand story that initially lured you into buying ETH or SOL? Big capital still believes in that story, and it never stops. So, what is my core argument? I argue that applying price-to-earnings ratios to smart contract public chains (the so-called "revenue meta-narrative") is essentially abandoning exponential growth. This means you've relegated the industry to a linear growth paradigm, that you believe 30 million daily active users and less than 1% of the total M2 money supply is the end—the crypto world is just one of countless industries, a marginal player. It hasn't won, nor is it inevitable. More importantly, I urge people to become its believers. Not just believers, but long-term believers. I firmly believe this exponential revolution will surpass any career you've ever been involved in. This is your "e-commerce revolution." When you're old and look back, you'll tell your children and grandchildren—I witnessed this upheaval. Not everyone believed that social systems could be restructured, or that programs running on our collectively owned decentralized computers could fundamentally change money and finance. But it did happen. It changed the world. And you were a part of it.

data.title: Arthur Hayes: Why are traditional financial derivatives all becoming perpetual contracts?
data.description:
Author: Arthur Hayes, Founder of BitMEX; Translated by: Jinse Finance

Less than a decade ago, those self-proclaimed high-flyers in traditional finance (TradFi) scoffed at BitMEX's 100x leveraged cryptocurrency derivatives market built using a new type of derivatives contract called perpetual contracts. When I read articles by Jim Grant of Grant's Interest Rate Observer and Dan Oliver of Myrmikan Capital pointing out that BitMEX offered such a "weapon of mass destruction" of cryptocurrency trading, I couldn't help but laugh. However, things have changed. Suddenly, traditional exchanges like the Singapore Exchange (SGX) and the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) are also launching perpetual contracts or similar products by the end of 2025.

In the Pax Americana retail space, calls for both ordinary citizens and the elite to trade perpetual contracts without VPNs prompted Coinbase to launch a "knock-off" version of perpetual contracts similar to the CBOE version earlier this year. In the waning Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) empire, other entrepreneurs daring to challenge its monopoly will have the opportunity to showcase their skills in a proposed regulatory sandbox managed by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). It is said that after cracking down on myself and others in the crypto derivatives space, the CFTC appears to have changed its tune and is once again a supporter of financial innovation. What exactly has made perpetual contracts (PERPs) a "fight or die" moment for traditional finance (TradFi)? Why is the trading volume of derivatives for all financial assets globally shifting from expiring futures and options contracts to perpetual contracts that never expire? To answer these questions, I will first review the history of perpetual contracts, explaining how BitMEX and I invented this new type of financial instrument. Then, I will delve into the fundamental principles of perpetual contracts and why they are better suited to the 24/7 internet world. Finally, I will look at the prospects for the widespread adoption of perpetual contracts beyond the cryptocurrency space and explain why traditional exchanges must adjust their product lines to include perpetual contracts and shared loss margin systems, or they will soon be replaced by centralized exchanges (CEXs) and decentralized exchanges (DEXs). Hyperliquid, the hot new decentralized exchange (DEX), launched the permissionless protocol HIP-3, allowing a company called XYZ to create perpetual contracts for Nasdaq 100 index stocks. This contract currently has a daily trading volume exceeding $100 million, making it one of the largest contracts traded by ADV on Hyperliquid. Stock perpetual contracts are poised to become the hottest product of 2026, with all DEXs and centralized exchanges (CEXs), including BitMEX, offering such products by the end of next year. This product will revolutionize the stock perpetual derivatives trading market, which has so far lagged behind in terms of market access and competition for diversified trading venues. If you invest in exchange tokens or stocks, then you should know everything about perpetual contracts.

Understanding History

If you want to conquer a foreign people, strip them of their history. When I was a child, my parents always supplemented the official narrative taught in school with other books. This wasn't because they had some kind of established, alternative version of known facts; rather, they encouraged me to question the official narrative and recommended books with various perspectives. Clearly, much of this academic research stemmed from the lack of positive, truthful narratives about "Black" identity in American history. And my parents would never allow me to become ignorant as the public and private school education systems expected.

Based on this, I offer firsthand information about the history of perpetual contracts.

Recently, many people have jumped in to comment on the importance and origins of cryptocurrency perpetual contracts (often simply called "perpetual contracts"). Given that they often tell my story with BitMEX, and that it's usually wrong, let's clarify some facts. Like many things in life, the BitMEX team drew inspiration from others when creating perpetual contracts and their associated margin system. I'm furious if anyone belittles our team's achievements, claiming it's just a design by Robert Shiller from the 1990s that was later popularized by BitMEX and the cryptocurrency industry. Screw that! That's pure nonsense. Read on to find out why. Let's go back to May 2016, when BitMEX was a five-person company. Ben Delo and Sam Reed were my two co-founders, and our initial two employees were Greg Dwyer (Business Development) and Jinming Shao (Trading Engine Engineer). The cryptocurrency derivatives market landscape then had both similarities and differences to today. OKCoin (now OKX) and Huobi (now HTX) dominated the cryptocurrency derivatives market. Together, these two exchanges held approximately 95% of the market share (by daily trading volume). BitMEX ranked third, which, frankly, was negligible. Despite having the highest leverage of any exchange (100x), the liquidity of our futures contracts paled in comparison to OKCoin and Huobi. At the time, the most liquid contract was a quarterly futures contract with Bitcoin margin. At BitMEX, we experimented with various methods to improve contract liquidity. We launched calendar spread contracts, daily contracts, weekly contracts, monthly contracts, and quarterly contracts. In short, we spread liquidity across various XBT/USD futures contracts, resulting in excessively thin liquidity. We often asked ourselves if there was a way to create a product without an expiration date, thus concentrating liquidity on a single contract. We also continuously considered how to simplify derivatives trading to better attract users transitioning from margin trading to futures trading. Margin trading is easy to understand because traders simply borrow funds and then trade on the spot order book. Understanding expiration dates and the price difference or basis between futures and spot is completely unnecessary for margin traders. However, these traders become confused when transitioning to futures trading. Since Ben, Sam, and I personally answer all customer support questions, we always try to reduce our daily workload to handle other tasks. Developing a product that looks and operates similarly to margin trading would save us the trouble of answering many confusing customer questions. The initial version of the perpetual contract was born to solve these two problems. Ben and I contacted several BitMEX staunch supporters in Hong Kong, all of whom were excellent financial engineers. We asked them (Joseph Wang and Bhavik Patel) for ideas on how to develop such a product. The final solution was very similar to the perpetual futures contract mentioned in a paper published by Robert Shiller in 1993. Our first perpetual contract product was based on daily updated USD and XBT funding rates, allowing long and short positions to exchange future cash flows. At the time, the mainstream exchange for cryptocurrency spot and margin trading was Bitfinex, which offered a highly liquid P2P lending market. Users could borrow and lend through these markets, which directly affected the interest rates paid by margin traders. This was the design concept behind our first perpetual contract launched in May 2016. The margin system used an insurance fund and combined it with a socialized loss mechanism called "dynamic profit equilibration"; we jokingly referred to internally as a "double penetration experience." We launched the XBTUSD perpetual contract, delisted the daily and monthly Bitcoin/USD futures, but retained the quarterly futures contract. Most clients were confused by this, and our support tickets skyrocketed. One of the most confusing issues for traders was the funding rate. They didn't understand the calculation method. Clients bombarded us on forums, our trollbox, and through support tickets. They demanded that we remove the culprit and follow OKCoin's example by offering the most liquid quarterly futures contracts. Internal divisions also emerged within the company: some advocated retaining existing products, while others championed reverting to offering only futures contracts. The chaotic situation was bad enough, but the rapid rise in Bitcoin prices presented another problem. The success of the Shiller model requires sufficiently dynamic domestic and foreign interest rates to influence market behavior. If these rates are not sensitive enough to market reactions, trading prices will exhibit significant discounts or premiums relative to spot prices—the so-called basis. In 2016, Bitcoin prices surged, making it difficult to find short sellers. In other words, there was a shortage of synthetic dollars used to purchase spot and leveraged Bitcoin on exchanges. Therefore, we needed to find ways to increase the supply of synthetic dollars on BitMEX. The observed market interest rate differential between the dollar and Bitcoin in the Shiller model can achieve this. If Bitcoin rises rapidly in dollar terms, the dollar interest rate should be higher than the Bitcoin interest rate. On the Bitfinex exchange, the daily yield of the dollar is 1%, while the yield of Bitcoin is much lower. This is indeed a significant difference, but when Bitcoin's daily gains reach 10% to 25%, the gains are not enough to attract arbitrageurs to engage in synthetic lending by selling USD and buying Bitcoin in a market-neutral manner. Since the interest rates for USD and Bitcoin are provided by external platforms, BitMEX cannot control these rates. Therefore, our swap prices have a high and rising premium relative to the spot price. The problem with an excessively large basis between the printed line and the printed point is that it causes problems with the margin system. To illustrate this, I will provide a simplified and extreme example. XBTUSD (Perpetual Contract) = $1000; XBT (Spot) = $500. Base Price = $500. For traders, you can choose to mark your position at the current underlying price or the spot price. If marked at the spot price, the long position will have $500 of unrealized loss, and the short position will have $500 of unrealized profit. To ensure long positions meet margin requirements, we must charge an additional $500 margin. If the price is marked at the spot price, long positions don't need to pay this extra margin, but if the spot price quickly falls back to the spot price, long positions will lose $500, and short positions won't profit. To avoid this shared loss, we choose to mark traders at the spot price. This results in higher margin requirements for long positions. This is not ideal, as it makes trading costs too high compared to competitors who don't require extra margin from long positions. I recognized our problem and began looking for solutions, but market conditions took time to allow for implementation. This market failure validated the views of those internally questioning whether we should continue offering futures contracts. I firmly believed this product was the future and savior of our exchange, but I had to find a solution, otherwise negative feedback would force us back to futures. My solution was to create a backtesting index that records the basis between swap contracts and spot contracts. Under certain constraints, this basis would become the funding rate for the next period. For example, if the average premium of a futures contract over the past eight hours is 1% of the spot contract, then a long position held at the funding timestamp will pay a 1% commission to a short position. To prevent immediate liquidation at the funding arrival timestamps (UTC 12:00, UTC 20:00, UTC 04:00), we use the following function to automatically rebalance unrealized and realized profits and losses and limit the funding arrival rate: Minimum (Funding Rate, 75% * Maintenance Margin or 0.5%) To adjust the sensitivity of the funding rate to the premium index, simply shorten or lengthen the funding rate settlement cycle. For example, if the funding rate is charged hourly, the maximum cumulative funding rate paid daily is 0.35% * 12 = 4.20% per day. Ben, Jinming, and I spent a week researching how to implement this new funding calculation method from the perspective of trading engines and market structure. Ben and Jinming quickly added the feature to our trading engine. The feature was ready, but extreme market malfunction forced us to launch it. Our terrible market performance was causing great dissatisfaction among our clients. Sam wanted to cancel swap trading, but I convinced him that if we launched a premium index, the market would recover on its own within days. For better or worse, BitMEX wasn't an Arthur Hayes-style dictatorship, but rather a consensus-managed team of Arthur, Ben, and Sam. I secured a stay of execution for the culprits and set about fixing the market in typical Arthur Hayes style. My strength wasn't subtlety, so I decided to implement a sudden change over a weekend in mid-June 2016, which would be the most effective course of action. I announced that in 24 hours, the funding mechanism would be changed to a retrospective premium index. At the time, perpetual contracts were trading at a high premium, but after the change was announced, long positions were liquidated as anticipation of large-scale funding was high, causing perpetual contract prices to immediately fall. Because the perpetual contract price was trading at a significant premium to the spot price, financing payments would increase substantially. By Monday morning, the perpetual contract price had become much closer to the spot price, and the market imbalance had eased. I told Sam to observe for another day; due to the impact of high interest rates, the basis would reach its lowest point. On Monday, when Ben, Jinming, and I went to the Lai Chi Kok office for snacks, the perpetual contract price had begun to stabilize. Fortunately, my prediction about establishing a self-correcting mechanism to adjust financing amounts based on the previous basis worked, and the calls within the company to cancel perpetual contracts disappeared. The only major subsequent change to the product design was the introduction of Automatic Deleveraging (ADL) functionality. We borrowed from Huobi's ADL mechanism but made some adjustments. The biggest problem with Huobi's ADL implementation was that traders could not predict the probability of their positions being liquidated prematurely, and once liquidated by ADL, traders would lose their entire position. Therefore, our innovation lay in creating a ranking system based on several factors and allowing partial ADL. The front-end interface displays a heat bar showing traders the probability of their positions being liquidated by ADL. Some ADL functions allow the exchange to liquidate a portion of positions, thus balancing the profits and losses of both long and short positions.

Over the next few months, we gradually discontinued all Bitcoin futures contracts, followed by other cryptocurrency futures contracts. Traders gradually became familiar with the trading style and began to enjoy the product. One day in late 2017, BitMEX surpassed OKCoin to become the most liquid derivatives exchange. By the end of 2018, BitMEX had become the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange in terms of spot and derivatives trading volume.

Everyone stands on the shoulders of giants. I want to list the existing technologies that enabled the birth of perpetual contracts as a sign of due respect.

ICBIT:

This was the first cryptocurrency derivatives exchange. They invented the inverse futures contract. This means that the margin and profit/loss currency (Bitcoin) are the same as the native currency. For example, each contract is worth $100 of Bitcoin at any price.

Regardless of the Bitcoin price, the contract's USD exposure is always $100. In Bitcoin terms, the exposure is 1/x. This is an unusual derivative, but it works because the exchange only accepts Bitcoin deposits. It was here that I first traded Bitcoin futures and simulated an inverse contract on BitMEX based on their design. Robert Shiller: As I explained earlier, he wrote a paper on perpetual futures contracts in 1993. The interest payments exchanged between long and short positions are determined by an exogenous pricing factor. I've never read his paper, but I believe his ideas influenced the advice we received from Joseph Wang and Bavic Patel. China Commodity Exchange: The concepts of socialized loss, ADL (anti-fraud loss), and insurance funds all originated here. 796 was the first Chinese cryptocurrency derivatives exchange to adopt a socialized loss mechanism. However, 796's technical architecture and understanding of complex derivatives were insufficient to handle the temptation of 50x leverage. Ultimately, due to its poor execution, the high weekly social loss tax rate allowed OKCoin and Huobi to exploit it and seize all of China's customers. To be fair, most cryptocurrency traders at the time couldn't correctly calculate the prices of quantitative and inverse Bitcoin futures contracts. When BitMEX was first established, we offered both types of futures contracts. Our anchor market makers (who are still one of the largest proprietary cryptocurrency trading firms today) suffered greatly from mispricing these contracts, even though I pointed out their calculation errors. Their eventual revenge was to withdraw from the exchange for three years after suffering heavy losses. OKCoin: BitMEX's first version of its margin system imitated OKCoin's social loss system. Huobi: Many features of Huobi inspired BitMEX's ADL margin system. Thanks for reading this history lesson on "perpetual contracts." But who cares? Why would an invention launched by a little-known Hong Kong exchange trigger a revolution, changing the way most financial assets are traded in derivatives? To understand this, I will analyze step by step why "perpetual contracts" are so attractive to retail traders. The perfect retail trading product. Retail investors, coupled with a socialized loss margin system, solve the two key issues of leverage and liquidity. Therefore, retail investors love retail investors. It is precisely because retail investors love retail investors that they threaten rent-seeking behavior on exchanges. Retail traders struggle to obtain high leverage because, in most jurisdictions, they lack access to the derivatives markets offered by exchanges. Therefore, they turn to informal platforms that offer highly leveraged Contracts for Difference (CFDs). When trading CFDs, traders face the platform directly, while when trading futures and futures contracts, clients trade on a transparent order book. If the platform is unregulated, it won't allow clients to enter and exit positions at reasonable prices. If traders want to avoid being exploited by CFD platforms, their only way to gain leverage is through options. This is why products like 0DTE options are so popular in many markets. These products have very high implied leverage. The problem with options is that contract payouts are not proportional to the price of the underlying asset like futures contracts. In the industry, futures are "delta 1" products. During my brief career in traditional finance, I was a delta 1 trader. Early on, due to my understanding of delta 1 derivatives, some of BitMEX's top traders were my colleagues, working at delta 1 trading desks across Hong Kong. Some would even dedicate their work hours to trading BitMEX's perps products on a single screen in the trading floor. To truly understand why perpetual contracts are so transformative, it's essential to delve into how margin systems work in traditional finance and cryptocurrencies. One reason traditional financial exchanges can't offer high leverage to retail investors is that their clearinghouses handle settlements. If a losing party goes bankrupt, the clearinghouse must have sufficient paid-up capital to pay winning parties. Therefore, exchanges will pursue all assets of any bankrupt trader through legal means. However, the situation is quite different in the cryptocurrency space. Cryptocurrencies are extremely volatile. This volatility, coupled with high leverage, makes large-scale liquidations highly likely. For us cryptocurrency exchange owners, recovering cryptocurrencies through legal means is impractical because the cryptocurrencies we must recover are bearer assets. In traditional finance, you don't own your financial assets; these assets are held by intermediaries. Therefore, courts can easily instruct banks (as government agencies, banks will always obey court rulings) to send your assets to traditional financial exchanges to repay your debts. Courts cannot instruct the Bitcoin blockchain to send Bitcoin from one address to another. Therefore, the margin system of cryptocurrency exchanges can only rely on initial margin to cover liquidation fees. Thus, a socialized loss mechanism combined with an insurance fund is absolutely necessary for the operation of cryptocurrency derivatives exchanges. Another reason traditional trading platforms cannot offer 100x leverage is the severe lack of collateral in their clearinghouses. A few years ago, I did some research on the market capitalization of the world's largest clearinghouses, and the results were shocking. Given that the cryptocurrency market operates 24/7, governments and central banks cannot manipulate it like they do with the stock, bond, and foreign exchange markets. For example, large fluctuations in Bitcoin prices, coupled with a large number of open contracts in the derivatives market, could destroy the world's largest exchanges. Therefore, the margin levels offered by traditional trading platforms for cryptocurrencies and other derivatives are far lower than those of their native cryptocurrency competitors. This is crucial when comparing the high-leverage (20x) stock futures contracts offered by Hyperliquid and BitMEX (coming soon) with the low-leverage stock futures contracts offered by other trading platforms. Retail investors will abandon stock derivatives on trading platforms in favor of the high-leverage stock futures products offered by cryptocurrency exchanges. The only way for traditional trading platforms to compete is to change the clearing model of the entire global derivatives market, but I don't think that will happen in the short term. BitMEX's leverage history is a good example. BitMEX initially used a guaranteed settlement margin system. Therefore, we could only offer 3x leverage. Compared to our Chinese competitors, insufficient leverage was the main reason why our trading volume was almost zero for the first nine months of operation. To compete, I changed the system to a loss-sharing system, combined with our advanced trading engine technology, and by October 2015, the leverage ratio had increased to 100x. Shortly thereafter, our trading volume soared, and the exchange became profitable. Cryptocurrency derivatives exchanges offer clients high leverage they can't get in traditional finance (TradFi), but at the cost of sometimes missing out on full profits if prices fluctuate too quickly. This is ideal for cryptocurrencies because without perpetual contracts, the cost of leverage using options would be prohibitively high. Nick Andrianov, chief trader at Maelstrom who previously led exotic derivatives trading at Deutsche Bank's Hong Kong branch, always points out that cryptocurrency options are inefficient for leveraged trading compared to perpetual contracts. He told me that a Bitcoin/USD call option with an implied volatility of 30 and a remaining term of one month would only yield 3.1 times the equity if the price of Bitcoin rose 10% in a single day. Let's compare this to trading a perpetual contract with 100x leverage. The initial margin is 1%; for example, you deposit 1 Bitcoin as margin to establish a position of 100 Bitcoins. A 10% increase in BTC in a single day means your unrealized profit is 10 BTC, or 10x return on equity. Trading options on highly volatile assets, even if the trade is correct, yields far less profit than trading highly leveraged physical stocks. Therefore, traders typically use physical stocks rather than options to gain leveraged exposure. Next, let's look at the next L – liquidity. Since perpetual contracts never expire, there's no need to spread liquidity across multiple futures contracts. The advent of perpetual contracts allowed BitMEX to concentrate liquidity on a single contract. This provides us with sufficient liquidity to challenge the dominance of OKCoin and Huobi, which spread liquidity across multiple quarterly futures contracts. Another advantage of having only one contract per cryptocurrency is that it's easier for retail traders to understand from a user interface/user experience perspective. Users don't need to judge which contract is best for their trading goals. There's only one option, and the liquidity is excellent. Once you become a believer, you must spread the gospel. And once perpetual contracts made BitMEX the world's largest exchange, it was time to attack other markets. To say that perpetual contracts and traditional finance aren't a perfect match would be a gross oversimplification. I firmly believe that for the vast majority of traders, and especially for the exchange itself, perpetual contracts are superior to futures and options contracts. This belief that I was selling a superior, safer product led me to naively believe I could convince regulators of its merits. I hoped BitMEX could monopolize the cryptocurrency trading market across all asset classes. I believed at the time that this would destroy the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME). I reached this conclusion by evaluating perpetual contracts versus traditional financial (TradFi) futures and options using the following variables: Collateral Usage and Security: Because cryptocurrency exchanges allow high-leverage trading, clients don't need to hold excessive collateral on the exchange. This isn't a problem for traditional financial exchanges where fiat currency margins are held in escrow by banks. However, for cryptocurrency exchanges, which are prone to hacking, clients must be highly vigilant about the amount and location of their collateral. If fiat stablecoins become the way clients fund their trading platform accounts in the future, the security of bearer blockchain assets will become a real issue even for traditional financial (TradFi) exchanges. They will understand why cryptocurrency exchanges prefer clients to minimize the amount of funds they hold on the exchange. Financial Security: Under a socialized loss margin system, cryptocurrency traders will at most lose their initial margin. No matter how bad their trading skills are (like James Wayne), losses on cryptocurrency exchanges won't affect their financial situation outside the platform. This is very different from traditional finance (TradFi), where speculators like James Wayne could lose their entire fortune because the exchange would pursue all their financial assets to pay off debts. Exchange Security and Competition: Because settlement is not guaranteed, exchanges can offer high-leverage trading on highly volatile assets. Users accept the fact that even profitable traders sometimes cannot capture all their profits, but this is a prerequisite for them to trade with 100x leverage. The number of exchanges is constantly increasing because they don't need the huge paid-up capital required to guarantee settlement like traditional clearinghouses. Exchanges compete to offer differentiated products. In traditional finance, most regions have only one national derivatives exchange, and government support and the large amount of capital used to support guaranteed clearinghouses make any competition difficult. By mid-2018, I believed that contracts backed by a socialized loss margin system were the safest way to offer highly leveraged derivatives trading to a large number of retail traders, so I thought it was time to meet with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and apply for a license. My goal at the time was to make BitMEX larger than the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME), which was, and remains, the world's largest derivatives exchange. This required us to enter and dominate the U.S. derivatives market. We partnered with the law firm Sullivan & Cromwell to arrange meetings with the CFTC's Labs and Markets division in Washington, D.C. The purpose of these two meetings was to determine if the CFTC was interested in processing our DCM and DCO license applications to bring BitMEX's contracts and our socialized loss margin system to the U.S. market. We met with two teams in the morning. They asked many insightful questions… I explained in detail how BitMEX operated, including malicious trading products and the shared loss margin system. A few weeks later, I learned that the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) was not interested in our license application. The matter stalled until more than two years later, when the U.S. Department of Justice and the CFTC filed criminal and civil lawsuits against us for violating the Bank Secrecy Act and the Commodity Exchange Act. I was incredibly naive to think the CFTC would appreciate my reasonable arguments. Like all regulatory agencies, the CFTC's role is to maintain the status quo. If they believe the current market structure is perfect enough, no amount of logic can convince them to allow competition. Global regulators are reluctant to allow genuine financial innovation because it could lead to the downfall of their domestic financial giants. It's worth mentioning that SBF exploited the mistakes we made at BitMEX—though he is not commendable. He and his politically connected parents were trying to achieve the same goal: bringing criminals to the U.S. to confront the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) head-on, and his method was: the American way—donating huge sums of money to politicians (primarily Democrats). When you ask people why they invested in FTX, or why they believed SBF would succeed, they mention that SBF's parents were professors at Stanford University and had close ties to the Democratic Party. I don't know the current situation, but they were one of the largest campaign fundraisers for the Democratic Party at the time. When we talk about campaign finance in the United States, the terminology we use carries a certain legitimacy. We talk about "donations" and "fundraising"; but when the West talks about other countries and their campaign finance systems, they talk about "bribery" and "corruption." Western investors blinded by SBF are euphemistically saying that SBF legally bribed "the right people." Some critics might argue that I'm just a sore loser, taking advantage of the other party's predicament to criticize them. But don't forget that by mid-2022, FTX was almost certain to obtain a banking license to issue its own stablecoin, as well as DCM and DCO licenses from the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). Looking at the image gallery below, you might think SBF is a reliable partner to those in power, even as his company attempts to destroy one of their largest corporate donors—the banking system. The stereotype is that a "white boy" walks into a lion's den and gets a hug; this "black boy" walks in and gets condemned. That's the reality; to understand more deeply how SBF uses stereotypes to his own advantage at the expense of his clients, investors, and global regulators, read my article, "White Boy". SBF and FTX.us donated nearly $100 million to his campaign, and if he weren't such a hopeless case of risk management and trading, FTX would be larger than Binance today. Wow, so cute. U.S. Representative Maxine Waters (SBF, right) served as chair of the powerful House Financial Services Committee from 2020 to 2022, during FTX's heyday. See, it has nothing to do with race. This is precisely the post-racist American future envisioned by Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. in his 1963 "I Have a Dream" speech.

Former US President Biden appointed Caroline Pham as a commissioner of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) in early 2022. The SBF certainly knows how to cultivate relationships with key figures. My closest encounter with a CFTC commissioner was at a cocktail party hosted by the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) in Boca Raton, Florida in 2018.

Money makes the world go round…

The FTX/Alameda incident dealt a heavy blow to the tolerance of US and overseas regulatory agencies and traditional finance (TradFi) towards cryptocurrencies. This is because the US Democratic Party's attitude towards cryptocurrencies shifted from ambivalence to outright hostility. Some political figures trusted SBF because of his prestigious family background; his parents were both heavyweight political figures, fitting the standard of a "white elite." To clear themselves of suspicion of collusion with SBF's scam, they began to try to prove that they had always opposed this fraudulent cryptocurrency scheme.

Therefore, from late 2022 to early 2025, US regulatory agencies launched a fierce crackdown on US cryptocurrency practitioners until President Trump was re-elected. The transformation of Trump and his family from cryptocurrency opponents to supporters deserves in-depth exploration. Trump's support for cryptocurrency was both a shrewd political strategy and a form of retaliation. It was a shrewd political strategy because the Democratic Party alienated a wealthy, active, and growing group. The transformative nature of cryptocurrency allowed SBF and other companies to amass billions of dollars in wealth in just a few years. America's cryptocurrency upstarts eagerly endorsed any political figure they deemed worthy of their support. Trump and his "Red Republican team" raised hundreds of millions of dollars from wealthy individual cryptocurrency donors, corporations, and political action committees (PACs). In 2024, this funding helped Trump overwhelmingly defeat Democratic presidential candidate Kamala Harris and helped the Republicans regain control of the House and Senate. This was one of the most glorious victories in modern American political history. This is retaliation because Trump's opponents broke the rules of the game by targeting his family's bank accounts after he left office in 2020. Trump is part of the establishment. Just look at his photos with celebrities in New York—the imperial capital—over the past 40 years. Trump's opponents crossed the line by launching criminal and economic attacks against him. Usually, when the elite clashed during the quadrennial presidential election fiasco, it was merely to show the public that Democrats and Republicans had different political platforms, when in reality, their common goal was to maintain the long-term stability of the elite. No matter what the previous president did, the next administration would always give him a break. Even Nixon was pardoned. But not Trump. After losing the 2020 election, he was convicted on various civil and criminal charges. To further damage Trump's defeat, Democrats closed the Trump family's bank accounts from 2021 to 2025. Trump initially dismissed cryptocurrencies with mere indifference, but now he and his family have finally grasped the dangers of banks. They have also recognized the necessity of a non-political hard currency like Bitcoin. Trump's embrace of Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies stems from desperation. If you listen to his sons, they explicitly state that one of the main reasons their family is fully committed to cryptocurrencies is their terrible experience with the banking system during Biden's presidency. Now that Trump is back, it's time to dismantle the financial system that nearly destroyed his family and business empire, because it is this system that allows cryptocurrencies to compete with traditional finance. Therefore, by 2025, traditional finance will either adapt to change or be rendered obsolete by cryptocurrencies and other innovative technologies. I review the history of cryptocurrency regulation in the United States for two reasons. First, most global traditional financial regulators blindly follow the United States like lemmings. This is a self-protective mechanism. No regulatory agency will lose its job for following U.S. financial regulations. But if an agency acts differently and suffers adverse consequences, it will certainly be ousted. Therefore, if the U.S. accepts cryptocurrencies for some reason, it will allow other regulatory agencies to do the same. For example, the Singapore Exchange (SGX) launched its own cryptocurrency immediately after Trump re-allowed traditional financial regulators to accept cryptocurrencies. Secondly, I want to use this history to demonstrate that this situation will not change, at least not until Trump's term ends in 2029. By then, the largest derivative of the S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100 will be perpetual contracts traded on cryptocurrency exchanges, not contracts traded on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME). By then, even if the next "peaceful emperor under American rule" opposes cryptocurrencies, their influence will be too great to be shaken. Before delving into how perpetual contracts will completely disrupt stock trading, I want to discuss their influence from the perspective of the wealth accumulation of a few typical perpetual contract holders. I believe Binance co-founder Chengpeng Zhao is one of the top ten richest non-political leaders in the world. He amassed such a massive fortune in less than a decade. I think CZ's wealth growth stems directly from Binance's rise to become the world's largest perpetual contract exchange after Bitcoin prices plummeted due to the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020, surpassing BitMEX. Binance elevated the popularity of perpetual contracts to a new level, and coupled with its dominance in altcoin trading, it created a cryptocurrency exchange giant. SBF created FTX after trading perpetual contracts for his hedge fund Alameda on BitMEX. Thanks to FTX's rapid growth (FTX essentially only offers perpetual contracts), SBF became a billionaire at the fastest pace in history (at least on paper). **Hyperliquid founder Jeff Yan** may already be a billionaire, or at least very close. He created the fastest-growing decentralized exchange (DEX), and if my stablecoin theory comes true, he could eventually become the largest exchange in human history. The next wave of billionaires in the cryptocurrency exchange space will come from the intersection of decentralized exchanges and stablecoin exchanges. **Stock Perpetual Contracts** Traditional financial groups (TradFi) are striving to maintain their dominance in stock trading. The public stock market is politically and economically crucial for vested interests. It will be very interesting to see how they respond to the rapid rise of stock perpetual contracts. These perpetual contracts will first dominate the offshore trading of US stock price risk. U.S. stocks, and indeed all stocks, will eventually be tokenized. But perpetual stock contracts don't need tokenization to succeed. Perpetual stock contracts already have fertile ground to grow. The U.S. stock market is the world's largest. Large U.S. tech companies like Nvidia have market capitalizations exceeding the annual GDP of most countries. Almost everyone globally uses their products. Yet, most retail investors cannot trade these stocks. These retail investors are accustomed to trading cryptocurrencies with high leverage anytime, anywhere. If Jaewon in Seoul could sell his Nvidia stock on the subway home from work, during his dead-end chaebol job, just like trading Bitcoin, he would certainly embrace it. This is the potential of perpetual stock contracts. Currently, daily trading volume for perpetual stock contracts exceeds $100 million. As traders and market makers become more familiar with the contract details, daily trading volume will soon reach billions of dollars. With a surge of breaking political, military, and economic news following the close of traditional financial markets on Friday nights, perpetual stock contracts will become a primary means for institutional and retail traders to hedge risk over the weekend. This will force major U.S. stock exchanges to implement 24/7 trading more quickly. Will it be cryptocurrency speculators or suited bankers who ultimately win the perpetual stock contract market? That depends on whether traditional financial regulators allow clearinghouses to offer socialized loss mechanisms. I predict that by the end of 2026, price discovery for the largest U.S. tech stocks and major stock indices (such as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100) will primarily occur in the retail-oriented perpetual contract market. I would be pleased if, in time, financial media outlets used the ticker symbols of S&P 500 component perpetual contracts as the best source of pricing information, rather than the Globex version from the CME Group. It's not too late; the CME and other exchanges have all the advantages, along with a large pool of smart and proactive employees. Perhaps they will see this article and reflect on it seriously. They have no reason to allow a group of cryptocurrency speculators to bypass intermediaries, especially when regulators are vying to champion exchanges. The Last Frontier The world's most traded derivatives contract is the CME Group's SOFR futures contract. Trading volume in the fixed-income market far exceeds that of the stock, forex, and cryptocurrency markets. My challenge to the cryptocurrency community is to create a completely new derivative that allows retail investors to speculate on interest rates in entirely new ways. The Pendle team is working on this, and their Boros protocol is rapidly gaining market acceptance. But this space remains full of opportunities, and many innovators can showcase their ideas here.

data.title: The x402 payment ecosystem is ushering in its own "Taobao moment".
data.description:
Author: Yue Xiaoyu; Source: X, @yuexiaoyu111

x402 payment ecosystem is ushering in its own "Taobao moment"!

1. What is the most important thing in building a payment ecosystem?

It is actually the merchant ecosystem, that is, you need to enrich the supply side first in order to attract more demand.

Analogous to the emergence of Alipay, the Taobao platform existed first, and Alipay was built to solve the payment problem.

In other words, electronic payment was rapidly popularized because of the emergence and prosperity of e-commerce platforms.

The popularity of Alipay is not because of its superior technology, but because Taobao's merchant ecosystem (supply) drove demand.

2. AI-native payment also faces the challenge of a cold start

The previously popular x402 is a very simple and pure AI-native payment protocol, mainly solving the problem of AI payment.

In developing its ecosystem, x402 faces the same challenges as when electronic payments first emerged: Where is the market? Who are the merchants? Who will supply the data? Now, the largest seller has appeared in the x402 payment ecosystem: the decentralized data provider Switchboard. Switchboard is an oracle and is currently the first and only decentralized data source in the x402 ecosystem that supports pay-per-use access. Simply put, x402 allows AI Agents to easily buy data, and Switchboard is the seller of high-quality, real-time data, using x402 to collect micropayments. 3. Switchboard is the "Taobao moment" of the x402 payment ecosystem! Just as Taobao first gathered a massive number of merchants, Switchboard first provided a rich and reliable supply of paid data, attracting the first batch of AI Agents to test it. Switchboard uses x402 to list all data sources for sale: AI Agent sends a request → Server returns a 402 error → Agent automatically makes on-chain payments → Data is obtained instantly. This forms a flywheel: Switchboard's supply attracts AI Agents (demand) → Transaction volume increases → More sellers follow x402 → Ecosystem prosperity. 4. In a payment ecosystem, supply must come first! Building a payment ecosystem requires first creating a marketplace like Taobao, enriching merchants and activating supply. x402 + Switchboard perfectly replicates Alipay + Taobao: the protocol provides the tools, and the supply side (data sellers) provides the market. a16z estimates the x402 market to reach $30T, and Switchboard, as a pioneering data marketplace, has the opportunity to capture a huge share of the data layer. We can continue to follow the latest developments in the x402 ecosystem.

data.title: Why are privacy coins rising against the trend when the crypto market is weakening?
data.description:
Author: Bradley Peak, Source: Cointelegraph, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • Prices of privacy coins like Zcash rose, while the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies and the price of Bitcoin saw a significant decline.

  • This rebound occurred against a backdrop of tightening policies, including pressure from the Financial Action Task Force (FATF), new EU anti-money laundering rules, and the increasing number of privacy coins being delisted.

  • Sanctions and prosecutions involving mixers and wallets have raised questions about the boundaries between infrastructure and money transfers, prompting compliance teams to take cautious de-risking measures.

  • Analysts are divided on this, with some seeing it as a protest against surveillance, while others see it as a surge in vulnerability at the end of the economic cycle amid shrinking high-risk sectors. Over the past six weeks, the cryptocurrency market capitalization has evaporated by more than $1 trillion, as traders have dumped speculative assets. Total market capitalization has fallen from a peak of over $4.3 trillion in early October to just over $3.1 trillion, a drop of approximately 25%-28%. Bitcoin's price has fallen nearly 30% from its all-time high of over $126,000 in early October and is currently trading just over $90,000. Against this backdrop, one of the strongest performing sectors, and also the most volatile, is privacy coins. Since late summer, Zcash's price has surged a hundredfold, with its market capitalization soaring from less than $1 billion in August to over $7 billion in early November. It briefly surpassed Monero to become the most valuable privacy coin. Meanwhile, Zcash rapidly climbed to the top of Coinbase's internal search rankings, with user search volume exceeding that of Bitcoin and XRP, indicating a rise in retail investor interest. Analysts say the combined effect of soaring prices and surging search volume foreshadows a typical hot trade. The complexity lies in the fact that this is happening in a market facing increasing regulatory pressure, exchange delistings, and sanctions-related scrutiny. Privacy Coins as Outliers: Data and Narratives The latest surge was clearly led by Zcash (ZEC), with Monero (XMR) lagging far behind. Key data cited by analysts includes: On some major trading platforms, ZEC has risen by over 200% in a month. From its late summer lows, ZEC's peer-to-peer volatility has achieved triple-digit percentage gains. XMR has also risen, but by a much smaller margin than ZEC, which briefly allowed ZEC's market capitalization to surpass XMR's. Despite the rebound, ZEC is still trading well below its all-time high. Explanations can be broadly categorized into two types: One focuses on structural and technical factors, including the reduced issuance as the halving progresses and the planned NU6.1 upgrade, which will transfer more control of funds to token holders. The other points to narrative and market structure, including overly high investor expectations for prices, regulatory concerns, a thin order book, and short squeezes in relatively small market segments. Most observers believe this rally comes at a time when regulatory and policy shifts are unfavorable to anonymous assets.

    Regulation is moving in the opposite direction

    Globally, privacy coins are entirely within the scope of anti-money laundering (AML) discussions.

    Since 2019, the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) has applied all of its AML and counter-terrorist financing (CFT) standards to virtual assets and virtual asset service providers (VASPs), including the "travel rule" requiring the inclusion of sender and recipient information in eligible transfers.

    A targeted update in 2024 found that approximately three-quarters of the jurisdictions assessed were still only partially or completely non-compliant with Article 15 recommendations, and approximately 30% had not yet legally implemented the travel rule. The FATF also specifically noted the concerning trend of illicit actors increasingly using cryptocurrencies that enhance anonymity.

    In Europe, the direction is clearer.

    According to legal and policy analysis, new EU-wide anti-money laundering rules, centered on Regulation 2024/1624 and related legislation, will ban the use of anonymous cryptocurrency accounts and privacy coins on licensed platforms by 2027. Crypto asset service providers will be required to implement bank-style anti-money laundering controls, verify the beneficial owners behind wallets interacting with their services, and gradually phase out support for fully anonymous tools. However, this does not mean that holding these assets is illegal everywhere. But it does mean that infrastructure is being redesigned in most regulated financial systems, predicated on privacy tokens being restricted or excluded. Delisting, shrinking trading venues, and liquidity risks: The regulatory environment has begun to reshape where and how privacy coins are traded. Key Shifts: In 2024, nearly 60 privacy coins were delisted from centralized exchanges, the highest number since 2021. Monero saw the most removals, with Dash (DASH) and several other cryptocurrencies also affected as exchanges re-evaluated their anti-money laundering policies. Binance restricted or canceled trading of XMR, ZEC, and DASH for users in several European jurisdictions, citing local regulations and compliance. Kraken announced at the end of 2024 that it would cease XMR trading and deposit services for European Economic Area (EEA) customers, with withdrawals ending at the end of the year, explicitly mentioning EU regulatory changes, including the Crypto Asset Markets (MiCA) framework. These measures could create typical liquidity problems. During market rallies, thinly traded markets can experience significant volatility due to relatively small inflows. As trading shifts from well-funded large platforms to smaller or less regulated ones, it becomes more difficult for large holders to exit the market without causing price volatility. The same structure can trigger sudden spikes or increase the risk of price "vacuums" during market downturns. Sanctions Spillover Effects, Court Proceedings, and Compliance Anxiety Sanctions and enforcement actions add another layer of uncertainty. In 2022, the U.S. Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) imposed sanctions on Tornado Cash, accusing the Ethereum-based mixer of laundering billions of dollars. In late 2024, a U.S. appeals court ruled that sanctioning immutable smart contracts exceeded the Treasury Department's authority. In March 2025, OFAC officially lifted sanctions against Tornado Cash. However, the legal risks did not disappear. Tornado Cash's developers faced criminal prosecutions in multiple jurisdictions, with one co-founder convicted of operating an unlicensed money transfer business. Another case involving Samourai Wallet sent a similar signal. In November 2025, its founder pleaded guilty in the United States and was sentenced to several years in prison for conspiring to operate an unlicensed money transfer company. Prosecutors alleged that over $2 billion in Bitcoin was transferred through the service. For compliance teams, the line between infrastructure and money transfer tools is difficult to draw. Some anti-money laundering service providers and policy agencies now classify privacy coins, mixers, and some high-risk decentralized finance (DeFi) tools in the same high-risk category. Under pressure from the FATF and national regulators, many companies tend to over-compliance, such as freezing deposits related to privacy tools, refusing listings, and restricting payment uses. For users, this presents secondary risks. Even if a particular cryptocurrency or protocol is not sanctioned, its surrounding ecosystem may still deem it too risky to engage with. Analysts are divided on what this surge signifies: Some see it as a protest trade against increasing on-chain surveillance, data-sharing rules, and sanctions screening. Others believe it's a speculative surge in the late stages of a shrinking segment, driven more by leverage and narrative than by long-term demand. Key policy milestones: EU anti-money laundering regulations restrict or effectively prohibit the issuance of privacy coins on authorized platforms, expected to be fully implemented around 2027. The FATF will continue to publish implementation review reports; the latest report states that compliance with virtual asset standards and the "travel rule" remains incomplete in most jurisdictions. On the technology front, upgrades like Zcash's NU6.1 fund transfer feature and experiments with optional privacy layers on major networks may test whether stronger privacy protections can coexist with regulators' requirements for traceability. Currently, privacy coins are caught between the long-standing debate on financial privacy and increasingly stringent global anti-money laundering and sanctions mechanisms. Understanding legal, liquidity, and enforcement risks is crucial to understanding how this field operates.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Crypto Industry Donates Over HK$40 Million to Support Tai Po Fire Emergency Efforts: Here’s How You Can Help
data.description:
Tai Po Blaze Sparks Rapid Crypto-Led Relief In Hong Kong

Flames engulfed Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po since 26 November, sweeping through eight high-rise residential blocks and affecting more than 1,900 flats.

At least four people are dead and three others are injured after a major fire engulfed a residential apartment complex in Hong Kong's Tai Po district on Wednesday, with photos and video from the scene appearing to show serious damage to several buildings. https://t.co/bXXM22GCfQpic.twitter.com/NmGhkKHcY0

— ABC News (@ABC) November 26, 2025

The blaze, classified as a No. 5 alarm which is the highest level in Hong Kong, has claimed at least 94 lives, including a firefighter, and injured 76, among them 11 firefighters.

Full-scale rescue operations are still on-going.

Authorities have traced the rapid spread of the fire to highly flammable styrofoam used in renovation works that have been underway since July 2024, shrouded in bamboo scaffolding and green mesh.

Three individuals connected to the contractor behind the renovations, two directors and a consultant, have been arrested for manslaughter, while separate graft investigations are underway regarding the HK$330 million repair project.

How Crypto Companies Moved Faster Than Traditional Institutions

As emergency services battled the blaze, Hong Kong’s crypto industry mounted a swift and visible relief effort.

Within hours, major exchanges announced donations running into millions of Hong Kong dollars to support rescue operations and aid affected residents.

Binance led the charge with HK$10 million, matched by local heavyweight HashKey.

Gate.io contributed HK$10 million, while KuCoin added HK$2 million, as confirmed through PAnews and Foresight News coverage.

與香港同在

Gate 已緊急設立 1,000 萬港幣援助基金，用於支援香港大埔火災受災居民的生活救助與社區重建。我們將根據實際需求分階段撥付、持續投入，讓援助抵達每一位受影響的居民…

— Gate 华语 (@Gate_zh) November 27, 2025

With HKD 2 million in support, KuCoin stands together with Hong Kong as the community recovers from the Tai Po incident.

Strength and care go hand in hand. 🖤

Details: https://t.co/gDQIO5cvdA#TogetherWeStand#HongKongpic.twitter.com/ASqqM5XhGJ

— KuCoin (@kucoincom) November 27, 2025

OKX has also stepped up, contributing HK$10 million to support urgent relief efforts and help Hong Kong recover.

Today, we stand with Hong Kong 🇭🇰

In moments of crisis, courage shows itself and Hong Kong is showing the world what strength looks like.

We’re contributing HK$10 million to support urgent relief efforts and help the city recover. We’re here and we’re committed. pic.twitter.com/XSkwKmPAXj

— OKX (@okx) November 27, 2025

Animoca Brands went further, opening dedicated crypto wallets to channel donations directly to the Hong Kong Red Cross and local charities.

The company invited global contributors to donate via EVM-compatible and Solana chains, pledging to convert all tokens into Hong Kong dollars and cover conversion fees, with the proceeds fully directed to relief efforts.

Yat Siu, co-founder and executive chairman of Animoca Brands, said,

“As a Hong Kong headquartered company, we are heartbroken by the tragedy and horror of the fire in Tai Po.

We encourage the global crypto community to help fund relief and recovery efforts for the thousands of people affected by this disaster, either by donating directly to relief organizations or via the wallets we have set up to facilitate crypto donations of liquid tokens.”

As of 28 November, the total pledged by the crypto sector exceeds HK$40 million (~US$5.14 million).

Justin Sun has also expressed support and an intention to donate, though no confirmed transaction or amount has yet been reported.

I am deeply saddened to learn about the recent fire in Tai Po, Hong Kong. My thoughts are with all the residents and families affected by this tragic incident.

To support the relief efforts, I will be making a donation to assist those who have suffered losses and to help with…

Crypto Response Shows Commitment to Community

data.title: XRP ETFs Ignite Institutional Frenzy as Bitcoin Reclaims $91K in Year-End Surge
data.description:
Bitcoin Reclaims $91K as XRP ETFs Rewrite the Institutional Playbook

Bitcoin’s (BTC) rebound toward $91,000 marks a decisive recovery from its recent dip to the $82,000 zone, with macro tailwinds — chiefly expectations of an imminent Federal Reserve rate cut — re-energizing market sentiment.

But in an unusual turn for a market normally dominated by Bitcoin-driven narratives, XRP ETFs have stolen the spotlight, accelerating past expectations and reshaping institutional allocation trends heading into the final month of 2025.

JUST IN: ETF clients buy $21.81 million worth of $XRP.

Total XRP assets held by ETFs - $676.49 million. pic.twitter.com/z9aGO7Y2Tg

— Whale Insider (@WhaleInsider) November 27, 2025

XRP investment products have seen $676 million in assets under management, buoyed by weekly inflows topping $230 million and daily volumes above $38 million.

Major players such as Bitwise, Canary Capital, and Franklin Templeton have contributed to the surge — not chasing momentum, but executing what analysts describe as a “durability-first strategy.” This means that institutions are now less concerned with short-term volatility and more focused on building exposure to regulated, long-horizon products calibrated for depth rather than speed.

XRP End-of-Day Recap — November 27, 2025

Quiet Moves, Loud Implications

XRP ETFs just inhaled $21.81M (Nov 27 est., via SoSoValue) — faster than a black hole. Bitwise led the surge, and institutional demand hasn’t eased for a second.

Mini Snapshot:
Cumulative XRP Spot ETF… pic.twitter.com/fUA63pdPkw

— CryptoDownUnder (@adoniscoxy) November 28, 2025

In contrast, the Bitcoin ETFs are getting less tailwind despite its price strength. Analysts suggest that this could be due to how the Bitcoin's ETF market tends to be more mature and deeply priced in, whereas XRP — newly legitimized by ETF availability — is experiencing its “initial institutional discovery phase.” That asymmetry helps explain why XRP ETFs are attracting heavy inflows at the same moment Bitcoin ETFs show tepid behavior.

Bitcoin, meanwhile, has stabilized above the psychological $90,000 threshold, rekindling mid-to-long-term bullish scenarios even as U.S. holiday conditions suppress spot market volumes. Ethereum, Binance Coin, and Solana have also tracked upward, while ADA lags behind after a period of underperformance.

Whale Movements, Derivatives Signals, and the Return of Risk Appetite

Beneath the surface of the price recovery lies a complex redistribution of liquidity. On-chain data shows that 700,000 BTC moved between long-term holders within 48 hours, while 9,000 BTC flowed onto exchanges in a single day. Rising average deposits on Binance — climbing from 12 BTC to 37 BTC per address — suggest a phase of strategic profit-taking and portfolio lightening, not capitulation. The market is repositioning rather than exiting.

Smart money is waking up.

In the last couple of weeks, 91 new whale wallets (100+ $BTC) appeared on-chain, while tiny wallets (0.1 BTC and below) keep vanishing.

In derivatives markets, sentiment is beginning to flip. Traders have largely abandoned protective put concentrations around $80,000–$85,000, shifting instead toward call targets at $100,000. Funding rates have turned positive again, signaling a renewed appetite for leveraged long exposure — a subtle but meaningful change after weeks of caution. Options desks describe it as “a breath returning to the market.”

ETF flows reinforce this reawakening. While Bitcoin products continue to see rotation rather than strong net inflows, multi-asset crypto ETFs and altcoin-specific products — including the newly approved Bitwise Dogecoin ETF — demonstrate that institutional allocators are widening their scope rather than retreating. Together, these signals point toward a year-end environment defined not by euphoria, but by strategic reinvestment driven by institutional structures, regulatory clarity, and shifting macro expectations.
